text: A type 304L stainless exposed to a fuel salt for 9.5 years in a type 304L stainless steel loop showed a maximum uniform corrosion rate of 0.86 mil/year.
Voids extended 10 mils into the matrix.
The corrosion resistance of maraging steel (12% Ni-5% Cr-3% Mo-bal Fe) at $662^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ was better than type 304L stainless but worse than Hastelloy N under equivalent conditions.
The uniform corrosion rate was 0.55 mil/year.
Subsurface voids were seen in the microstructure of the specimens after 5700 hr, and microprobe analysis disclosed a definite depletion of chromium and iron.
Type 316 stainless steel exposed to a fuel salt in a type 316 stainless steel loop showed a maximum uniform corrosion rate of 1 mil/year for 4298 hr.
Table 1 gives the alloys involved, the salt compositions, and the details of the tests.

It melts at $1035^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ $(1895^{\circ}\mathrm{F})$ , but its freezing point is markedly depressed by useful diluent fluorides.
Uranium trifluoride disproportionates at temperatures above $\sim 1000^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ by the reaction

It is chemically unstable $^{2,3}$ at lower temperatures in most molten fluoride solutions and is tolerable in reactor fuels only with a large excess of $\mathrm{UF_4}$ so that the activity of $\mathbf{U}^0$ is so low as to avoid appreciable reaction with moderator graphite or container metal.
It melts at $1111^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ $(2032^{\circ}\mathrm{F})$ , but fortunately its freezing point is markedly depressed by fluoride diluents which are also useful with $\mathrm{UF}_4$ .
Consideration of nuclear properties alone leads one to prefer as diluents the fluorides of Be, Bi, $^{7}\mathrm{Li}, \mathrm{Mg}, \mathrm{Pb}$ , and $\mathrm{Zr}$ in that order.
This leaves $\mathrm{BeF}_2$ and $^{7}\mathrm{LiF}$ as the preferred diluent fluorides.
The binary system $\mathrm{LiF - BeF_2}$ has melting points below $500^{\circ}C$ over the concentration range from 33 to 80 mole $\% \mathrm{BeF}_2$ .
2 is characterized by a single eutectic (52 mole $\% \mathrm{BeF}_2$ , melting at $360^{\circ}C$ ) between $\mathrm{BeF}_2$ and 2LiF $\cdot \mathrm{BeF}_2$ .
The compound 2LiF $\cdot \mathrm{BeF}_2$ melts incongruently to LiF and liquid at $458^{\circ}C$ .
LiF $\cdot \mathrm{BeF}_2$ melts below $280^{\circ}C$ .
The phase behavior of the $\mathrm{BeF}_2\text{-UF}_4^{6,7}$ and $\mathrm{BeF}_2\text{-ThF}_4^3$ systems is very similar.
The binary diagrams $\mathrm{LiF - UF_4}^9$ and $\mathrm{LiF - ThF_4}^{10}$ are generally similar and much more complex than the binary diagrams just discussed.

congruently) and a single eutectic at 27 mole $\%$ UF $_4$ , melting at $490^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .

The LiF-ThF $_4$ system contains four binary compounds, one (3LiF·ThF $_4$ ) which melts congruently, with two eutectics at $570^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and 22 mole $\%$ ThF $_4$ and at $560^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and 29 mole $\%$ ThF $_4$ .
The ternary system $\mathrm{LiF - ThF_4 - UF_4}$ ,<sup>11</sup> shown in Fig.
3, has no ternary compounds and a single eutectic freezing at $488^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ with 1.5 mole $\%$ $\mathrm{ThF_4}$ and 26.5 mole $\%$ $\mathrm{UF_4}$ .
Figure 4 gives the ternary system LiF-BeF $_2$ -ThF $_4$ ; this system shows a single ternary eutectic at 47 mole % LiF and 1.5 mole % ThF $_4$ , melting at $360^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .

The system is complicated to some extent by the fact that the compound 3LiF·ThF $_4$ can incorporate $\mathrm{Be}^{2+}$ ions in both interstitial and substitutional sites to form solid solutions whose compositional extremes are represented by the shaded triangular region near that compound.
Liquidus temperatures $< 550^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ( $1022^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ ) are available at $\mathrm{ThF_4}$ concentrations as high at 22 mole %.
The maximum $\mathrm{ThF_4}$ concentration available at liquidus temperatures of $500^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ( $932^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ ) is above 14 mole %.
Inspection of the diagram reveals that a considerable range of compositions with $>10$ mole % $\mathrm{ThF_4}$ will be completely molten at or below $500^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
As expected from the general similarity of $\mathrm{ThF_4}$ and $\mathrm{UF_4}$ - and especially from the substitutional behavior shown by the LiF- $\mathrm{UF_4}$ -ThF $^4$ system (Fig.
3) - substitution of a small quantity of $\mathrm{UF_4}$ for $\mathrm{ThF_4}$ scarcely changes the phase behavior.
4 represents the behavior of the LiF-BeF2-ThF4-UF4 (MSBR fuel) system over concentration regions such that the mole fraction of $\mathrm{ThF_4}$ is much greater than that of $\mathrm{UF_4}$ .

Table 2 records pertinent data for the major components of MSRE and MSBR fuels and for corrosion products in molten 2LiF·BeF<sub>2</sub>.

For the case where the total uranium content of the salt is 0.9 mole % (as in the Molten-Salt Reactor Experiment), the activity of metallic uranium (referred to the pure metal) is near $10^{-15}$ with $1\%$ of the $\mathrm{UF_4}$ converted to $\mathrm{UF_3}$ and is near $2 \times 10^{-10}$ with $20\%$ of the $\mathrm{UF_4}$ so converted. $^{13}$ Operation of the reactor with a small fraction (usually $< 2\%$ ) of the uranium present as $\mathrm{UF_3}$ is advantageous insofar as corrosion and the consequences of fission are concerned.
Operation with $>50\%$ of the uranium as $\mathrm{UF_3}$ would lead to much more concentrated (and highly deleterious) alloying and to formation of uranium carbides.
2 \mathrm {U F} _ {4} (\mathrm {d}) + \mathrm {M} (\mathrm {c}) \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm {U F} _ {3} (\mathrm {d}) + \mathrm {M F} _ {2} (\mathrm {d}), \tag {7}
Reactions (8), (9), and (10) will proceed essentially to completion at all temperatures within the MSBR circuit.

Reaction (7) with $\mathrm{UF}_4$ , on the other hand, may have an equilibrium constant which is strongly temperature dependent; hence, when the salt is forced to circulate through a temperature gradient, a possible mechanism exists for mass transfer and continued attack.
Reaction (7) is of significance mainly in the case of alloys containing relatively large amounts of chromium.

In some melts (NaF-LiF-KF-UF $_4$ , for example) the equilibrium constant for reaction (7) with chromium changes sufficiently as a function of temperature to cause formation of dendritic chromium crystals in the cold zone.
For the blanket and fuel salts, the oxides and sulfides were removed by gas sparging for several hours (usually 10 to 20) at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ with an anhydrous mixture of HF in hydrogen (1:4).

Gas sparging of the melt at $700^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ with hydrogen alone for several hours (usually 20 to 40) was used as the final phase of the purification process to reduce $\mathrm{FeF_2}$ and $\mathrm{NiF_2}$ concentrations to low values and to remove any residual HF:
The Hastelloy N material for loops 15, 16, 18, 19, and 21 was from heat No.
5097 obtained from Stellar Division, Cabot Corp, and the composition is given in Table 3.
The loops were fabricated from 0.750-in.-OD annealed tubing with a wall thickness of 0.072 in.
Machined pieces were annealed at $1175^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ in $\mathbf{H}_2$ before welding.
The material was TIG welded in conformance with ORNL Specifications PS-23 and PS-25 and inspected under ORNL Specification MET-WR-200.
After welding, the loops were stress relieved at $880^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ for 8 hr.
Before filling with salt, the loops were degreased with ethyl alcohol, dried, and then heated to $150^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ under vacuum to remove any traces of moisture.

The procedure for filling the loops consisted of heating the loop, the salt pot, and all connecting lines to approximately $550^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and applying helium pressure to the salt supply vessel to force the salt into the loop.
Normally the first charge of salt was held for $24\mathrm{hr}$ in the loops at the maximum operating temperature and then dumped.
Helium cover gas of $99.998\%$ purity and under slight pressure (approx 5 psig) was maintained over the salt in the loops during operation.

With the exception of loop 1258 which only had specimens in the hot leg, the loops all contained 14 to 16 specimens $0.75 \times 0.38 \times 0.030$ in., each with a surface area of 0.55 in. $^{2}$ ( $3.45\mathrm{cm}^{2}$ ).

(Modifications to the standard Hastelloy N composition were made to test for improved mechanical properties after neutron irradiation.) The composition of the standard and modified Hastelloy N specimens is given in Table 4.
Eight specimens were attached at different vertical positions on each of two $\frac{1}{8}$ -in. rods.
Loop 15 was operated to determine the compatibility of a proposed blanket salt for a two-fluid molten salt breeder reactor with Hastelloy N.
Figure 6 shows loop 15 on the left in place before operation.
For loop 15 the fuel pot and associated lines had to be heated to $730^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and higher than normal inert gas pressure applied to fill the loop with salt.
After repairs, we managed to fill the loop with the flush charge but found it impossible to initiate circulation even after heating the loop's hot leg to $760^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
The loop was operated at a maximum temperature of $676^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ with a temperature difference of $54^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
A helium cover gas under slight positive pressure ( $\sim$ 5 psig) was maintained over the salt in the loops during operation.
On May 1, 1968, after 2003 hr of uneventful operation, the loop cooled due to a blown fuse, and all salt was frozen.
Heat was applied to the loop in order to thaw the salt, and a rupture occurred in the hot leg loop piping 30 in. down from the hot leg surge tank (Figs.
7 and 8), thus ending the operation of loop 15.
We attribute this increased corrosion resistance to the lower iron concentration of the titanium-modified alloy (Table 4).
Weight changes of Hastelloy N specimens in loop 15 vs time of operation in LiF-BeF $_2$ -ThF $_4$ (73-2-25 mole%).
The chromium and iron increased with time, but the changes were very small (about 20 ppm increase).

Nickel showed a small overall increase, molybdenum remained at $< 20$ ppm during operation, oxygen ranged from $< 50$ to a high of 244 to a value of 75 ppm just before the rupture, and water was $< 500$ ppm in each analysis.
Table 5 gives pertinent details of loops that had operated in past years for various times with LiF-ThF $_4$ (71-29 mole %) salt.

The two Inconel loops that operated for a year (8760 hr) showed subsurface void formation for distances up to $6^{1/2}$ mils, but the INOR-8 (Hastelloy N) loop that operated for a year showed no voids.
The improved behavior of INOR-8 was somewhat expected since there is less chromium present.
The first problem was the high temperature $(730^{\circ}\mathrm{C})$ needed to transfer the fuel and the apparent presence of unmelted salt constituents.

For a total specimen exposure time of $37,792\mathrm{hr}$ , the maximum weight loss was $4.7\mathrm{mg/cm^2}$ , equivalent to a uniform corrosion rate of $0.05\mathrm{mil/year}$ , and the maximum weight gain was $4.2\mathrm{mg/cm^2}$ .
Figure 11 shows the microstructure of the hottest and coldest specimens.

Loops 18 and 19 were constructed of standard Hastelloy N with removable specimens in each leg and were operated with the salt mixture $68\%$ LiF- $20\%$ $\mathrm{BeF}_2 - 11.7\%$ $\mathrm{ThF}_4 - 0.3\%$ $\mathrm{UF_4}$ (mole%).
Loop 19 had a molybdenum hot finger containing bismuth at the bottom of the hot leg (Fig.
12) which allowed contact between the flowing salt and the bismuth.
After a power outage at 4741 hr the bismuth finger on loop 19 was inadvertently heated to temperatures over $1000^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
Prior to the 4741-hr shutdown the maximum weight loss for a standard Hastelloy N specimen at $704^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ was $0.8\mathrm{mg/cm}^2$ .
Assuming uniform dissolution this weight loss is equivalent to 0.07 mil/year, an insignificant corrosion rate.
The chromium content of the salt increased from 25 to 66 ppm while iron decreased from 78 to 23.
Figure 13 shows the metallographic appearance of the hottest and coldest
Microstructures of Hastelloy N specimens from loop 15A after 37,792 hr exposure to LiF-BeF $_2$ -ThF $_4$ (73-2-25 mole %).
(a) Hot leg, $676^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ; (b) cold leg, $613^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
Microstructure of Hastelloy N from loop 19 exposed to LiF-BeF $_2$ -ThF $_4$ -UF $_4$ (68-20-11.7-0.3 mole %) 4741 hr.
(a) Hot leg, $700^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ; (b) cold leg, $550^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
The bismuth content of the salt was $< 20$ ppm for the last 1500 hr.
After the temperature excursion the salt and the bismuth at the top of the finger were both analyzed for certain metal impurities, and the results are given in Table 6.
Table 6 shows that bismuth extracted impurities from the salt in quantities proportional to the solubility of these structural metals in bismuth.
Analysis of the dumped salt from loop 19 disclosed 225 ppm bismuth.

At the time of this analysis, in loop 18 the maximum weight loss of a standard Hastelloy N specimen was $0.8\mathrm{mg/cm}^2$ at $704^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ in 3400 hr, a very low rate, equivalent to 0.08 mil/year assuming uniform attack.
The chromium concentration in the salt in this loop had increased from 21 to 90 ppm.
The specimen from the hottest position of loop 19 was bent around a $\frac{1}{4}$ -in.-diam tube and examined metallographically (Fig.

Microstructure of stressed Hastelloy N specimen from loop 19 exposed to LiF-BeF $_2$ -ThF $_4$ -UF $_4$ (68-20-11.7-0.3 mole %) for 4741 hr at $700^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
Repairs were made to loop 19 and the molybdenum finger, and the system was restarted with fresh salt and bismuth with the loop designated as 19A.
Loop 19A operated 24,515 hr, and the weight changes are shown in Fig.
Assuming uniform loss the maximum weight loss was equivalent to only 0.02 mil/year.
A modified Hastelloy N alloy (Ni-13.0% Mo-8.5% Cr-0.1% Fe-0.8% Ti-1.6% Nb) lost less weight than a standard alloy at the same position.
We attribute this difference to the low iron in the modified alloy, compared with about 5% in the standard alloy.
The chromium content of the salt increased 194 ppm in 18,000 hr, while iron decreased 70 ppm, and there was no detectable bismuth in the salt.
During the last exposure period, the specimens in the hot leg were inadvertently placed 4 in. lower than usual.

As we had surmised earlier in loop 19, the bismuth in contact with the salt appeared to have had no effect on our mass transfer results as loops 18 and 19 showed about the same amount of mass transfer (Fig.
Loop 18 operated a total of 15,930 hr, and the maximum weight loss was $1.5 \, \mathrm{mg/cm}^2$ at $704^\circ \mathrm{C}$ , equivalent to a corrosion rate of 0.05 mil/year.
Chromium in the salt increased from 20 to 190 ppm while iron decreased from 80 to 20 ppm.
Weight changes of Hastelloy N from loop 19 exposed to LiF-BeF $_2$ -ThF $_4$ -UF $_4$ (68-20-11.7-0.3 mole %) for various times.
Loop 21, a Hastelloy N thermal convection loop (Fig.
17) with removable specimens in each leg and containing the Molten-Salt Reactor Experiment reference fuel salt (Table 1), operated for 13,798 hr.
The loop was equipped with electrochemical probes which measured the $\mathrm{U}^{3+}/\mathrm{U}^{4+}$ ratio, and hence provided an instantaneous measure of the oxidation potential of the salt.

The electrochemical probes passed through mechanical fittings (separated from the metal by Teflon), which were on risers 10 in. from the surge tank (to protect the Teflon from the heat of the salt) and then entered the salt in the large hot leg surge tank, as shown in Fig.

The loop was filled on July 20, 1971, and held under isothermal conditions at about $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ until July 26, at which time the cold leg temperature was established and Hastelloy N specimens were inserted into both the hot and cold leg of the loop for corrosion studies.
Analyses of the $\mathbf{U}^{3+}$ concentration of the melt were started on July 22 and were continued on a routine basis until the specimens were removed from the loop on August 23.
The original measurements in the melt indicated that about $0.02\%$ of the total uranium was present as $\mathrm{U}^{3+}$ .
The $\mathrm{U}^{3+}$ concentration showed a gradual increase until a value of $0.05\%$ was reached at 475 hr of loop operation.
At this point the rate of formation of $\mathrm{U}^{3+}$ increased, and the concentration reached a value of about $0.15\%$ when the metal specimens were removed from the loop.
The maximum weight loss of a hot leg specimen was $0.2\mathrm{mg/cm}^2$ , and the maximum weight gain of a cold leg specimen was $0.05\mathrm{mg/cm}^2$ .
The electrode was encased in a $\frac{1}{2}$ -in. nickel tube, open at the bottom, which protruded below the surface of the melt.
The computer then operated the voltammeter to perform five analyses for $\mathbf{U}^{3+}$ , and the results plus some diagnostic information were printed out on a Teletype after each analysis.
At the end of the run the average $\mathbf{U}^{3+}$ concentration and standard deviation were calculated and printed out.

As an example of the precision of the analysis system, a standard deviation of $2.1\%$ was obtained for the results of the hourly analyses made over a one-weekend period when the $\mathbf{U}^{3+}$ concentration appeared to be relatively stable.
The percent of the total uranium which was present as $\mathbf{U}^{3+}$ for the first 3600 hr of loop operation is shown in Figs.
The irregular appearance of the data at 600 hr was due to large temperature fluctuations in the electrode tank.
The first measurements with the shielded electrode were made at 1200 hr and are plotted as the squares in Figs.
At about 1250 hr it was first noted that analyses with the shielded and unshielded electrodes gave different values of the $\mathbf{U}^{3+}$ concentration.
At 2350 hr the nickel tube shield was raised out of the melt, and the measurements with the two electrodes were in agreement as shown in Fig.
The second time the specimens were removed was November 29, 1971.
The specimens had been exposed a total of $2513\mathrm{hr}$ , and the maximum weight loss was $0.2\mathrm{mg/cm^2}$ while the maximum weight gain was $0.05\mathrm{mg/cm^2}$ .
The last time the specimens were removed was February 8, 1972.
The total exposure time was $4169\mathrm{hr}$ , and the maximum weight loss was $0.23\mathrm{mg/cm^2}$ , which would correspond to a corrosion
rate of 0.02 mil/year, assuming uniform material removal.
The chromium content of the salt increased 40 ppm.

Except for the first time, when the $\mathbf{U}^{3+}$ concentration was already at a low value, the insertion of the metal specimens appeared to introduce an oxidant which caused a decrease in the $\mathbf{U}^{3+}$ concentration (1316 hr on Fig.
Special precautions were taken for the next specimen insertion to determine if this operation could be performed without oxidizing the $\mathbf{U}^{3+}$ .
At 1725 hr the shielded electrode, which had previously been removed for examination, was put back into the loop without immersing it into the salt.
20, the $\mathbf{U}^{3+}$ concentration decreased and then rapidly recovered, probably due to dilution effects caused by the salt flow.

The reason for the experiment was the discovery of cracks in stressed Hastelloy N that had been exposed to fuel salt in the MSRE, $^{18}$ and subsequent evidence that tellurium entering salt as a fission product could cause cracks of this nature.

The test bed for this experiment was a Hastelloy N thermal convection loop that had previously operated $\sim 38,000$ hr as loop 16A with the fuel mixture $65.5\%$ LiF $-34\%$ BeF $_2$ $-0.5\%$ UF $_4$ (mole%).
Operating conditions for loop 16A are noted in Table 1.
For the initial experiment (which lasted 3400 hr), a Hastelloy N specimen coated with $1.6\mathrm{mg/cm}^2$ tellurium was added to the normal complement of standard Hastelloy N specimens.
In the first $1300\mathrm{hr}$ of operation the weight loss of the tellurium-coated specimen equaled the amount of tellurium coated on the specimen.
In the last $2100\mathrm{hr}$ the weight loss of the tellurium-coated specimen was the same as the hottest
Thus, all the tellurium was apparently removed in the first 1300 hr.
The isotope used was $^{127\mathrm{m}}\mathrm{Te}$ , and it was electroplated on the specimens.
The specimens were removed after 42, 159, 396, 701, 1318, and 1792 hr exposures.
After the first $42\mathrm{hr}$ in the loop, all specimens lost at least $66\%$ of their initial activity.
Our standard specimen which had remained at room temperature in air lost $4\%$ of its activity.
After $159\mathrm{hr}$ in the loop, all specimens had lost $75\%$ of their initial activity.
The standard specimen had lost $8\%$ .

Loop 1258, constructed of type 304L stainless steel (11.07% Ni, 18.31% Cr, 0.028% C, bal Fe) and containing removable corrosion specimens in the hot leg, operated nine years (80,000 hr) and thus provided long-term stainless steel-fuel salt compatibility data.

An LiF-BeF $_2$ -ZrF $_4$ -UF $_4$ -ThF $_4$ (70-23-5-1-1 mole %) salt circulated in the loop at a maximum temperature of $688^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and a minimum temperature of $588^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
The loop was started July 29, 1963.
Four of ten specimens were removed October 31, 1963, after 2244 hr exposure and are pictured in Fig.
The weight losses were 11.6, 11.3, 8.1, and $3.3\mathrm{mg/cm}^2$ .
In terms of uniform material removal, the maximum corrosion rate was over 2 mils/year.
Figure 24 shows a micrograph of a specimen removed after 15,000 hr at $680^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
We see a maximum of 2 mils grain boundary penetration.
In January 1967 after approx 30,000 hr, the remainder of the original specimen set was removed, and a second set, consisting of ten new specimens, was placed in the hot leg.
The hottest specimen was replaced after 3700 hr, and the rest of the specimens remained in the loop (periodic removals for examination) until its shutdown.
The specimen exposed at the highest temperature, $688^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , was removed for detailed metallurgical analysis after 5700 hr (between May 1967 and February 1968).
Figure 25 shows all the specimens at this time (9400 hr exposure).
Voids extend into the matrix for a maximum distance of 2.5 mils along grain boundaries.
Note that the chromium concentration of the alloy is decreased for a distance of $28\mu \mathrm{m}$ .
Microstructure of type 304L stainless steel specimen exposed to LiF-BeF $_2$ -ZrF $_4$ -ThF $_4$ -UF $_4$ (70-23-5-1-mole %) for 15,000 hr at $680^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .

Type 304L stainless steel specimens from loop 1258 exposed to LiF-BeF $_2$ -ZrF $_4$ -ThF $_4$ -UF $_4$ (70-23-5-1-1 mole %) for 9400 hr at temperature ranges from $688^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ (top specimen) to $668^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ (bottom specimen).
After 62,600 hr operation of the loop (September 1970) we replaced the heaters and thermocouples of the loop.

Chromium and iron concentration gradient in a type-304L stainless steel specimen from loop 1258 exposed to $\mathrm{LiF - BeF_2 - ZrF_4 - ThF_4 - UF_4}$ (70-23-5-1-1 mole %) for 5700 hr at $688^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ;
The weight changes of the second specimen set after 32,000 hr are seen in Fig.
The microstructures of three specimens after 45,724 hr salt exposure are shown in Fig.
Voids extend for at least 10 mils into each specimen.
At loop shutdown the second specimen set had been exposed to the salt for 49,057 hr.
The maximum weight loss was 95.9 mg/cm², equivalent to a uniform corrosion rate of 0.86 mil/year.
Because of interest in iron-base alloys with lower chromium contents for possible containment of molten salts, we tested a maraging steel (12% Ni-5% Cr-3% Mo-bal Fe) specimen in loop 1258.
The specimen was annealed 1 hr at $800^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and air cooled.
Table 7 compares the weight loss and corrosion rate of this specimen with type 304 stainless steel and Hastelloy N under similar conditions of exposure.
Figure 30 shows the metallographic appearance of the maraging steel specimen after exposure for 5700 hr at $662^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .

Using an accelerating voltage of $20\mathrm{kV}$ and specimen current of $0.01\mu \mathrm{A}$ , x-ray intensities were recorded (Fe $K\alpha$ , Ni $K\alpha$ , Cr $K\alpha$ , and Mo $L\alpha$ ) in both as-received and corroded specimens.
Table 8 shows the comparison for each of the four elements.
Figure 31 shows the cathode ray tube display and corroborates the quantitative data.

Loop 22 was a type 316 stainless steel thermal convection loop which operated for 4491 hr with a $\mathrm{LiF - BeF_2 - ThF_4 - UF_4}$ salt mixture identified in Table 1.
The harp portion of the loop was 1-in.-OD tubing with 0.109-in. wall thickness.
The material was from heat X-22662, U.S.
National Tube, and the ORNL Inspection Engineering Number was IR-9141, Y-12 No.
The nominal composition of the alloy was $16.6\%$ Cr, $13.6\%$ Ni, $2.25\%$ Mo, bal Fe.
Figure 33 shows the microstructure of the hottest specimen after 1410 hr.
Subsurface voids are seen for a distance of 1 mil.
After 2842 hr exposure the specimen at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ had lost a total of $6.2\mathrm{mg/cm}^2$ , equivalent to a uniform corrosion rate of 0.96 mil/year.
Weight changes of type 316 stainless steel specimens in loop 22 exposed to $\mathrm{LiF - BeF_2 - ThF_4 - UF_4}$ (68-20-11.7-0.3 mole %) as a function of position and temperature.
Microstructure of type 316 stainless steel specimen in loop 22 exposed to LiF-BeF $_2$ -ThF $_4$ -UF $_4$ (68-20-11.7-0.3 mole %) after 1410 hr at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .

The main corrosion reaction in this system is $2\mathrm{UF}_4 + \mathrm{Cr} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{CrF}_2 + 2\mathrm{UF}_3$ ; thus, in order to determine if we could decrease the mass transfer of the stainless steel, we added $\mathrm{UF}_3$ to the salt after the 2842-hr exposure measurements to make the salt less oxidizing.
In the next 645-hr time period the mass transfer was somewhat less, and the overall corrosion rate was 0.83 mil/year.
In the final 811-hr time period, the weight loss of the hottest specimen increased such that the uniform corrosion rate was $>1$ mil/year.
The average uniform corrosion rate for the hottest specimen was 1 mil/year for the entire 4298-hr period of loop operation.
Figure 34 shows the microstructures of hottest and coldest specimens at the end of the run.

All corrosion specimens were annealed $1\mathrm{{hr}}$ at ${1121}^{ \circ }\mathrm{C}$ except Monel,which was annealed $1\mathrm{{hr}}$ at ${800}^{ \circ }\mathrm{C}$ .
The cross-section view of the Inconel 606 is unusual since at one end of the specimen we see heavy attack while at the other end almost no attack.

The attack of the Inconel 601, Inconel 690, and the Incoloy 811E was more straightforward and resulted in large areas of void formation: 5 mils for Inconel 601, 15 mils for Inconel 690, and 18 mils for Incoloy 811E.

A type 304L stainless steel exposed to a fuel salt for 9.5 years in a type 304L stainless steel loop showed a maximum uniform corrosion rate of 0.86 mil/year.
Voids extended into the matrix for 10 mils.
The corrosion resistance of a maraging steel (12% Ni-5% Cr-3% Mo-bal Fe) at $662^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ was better than type 304L stainless but worse than Hastelloy N under equivalent conditions.
The uniform corrosion rate was 0.55 mil/year.
Voids were seen in the microstructure of the specimens after 5700 hr, and microprobe analysis disclosed a definite depletion of chromium and iron.
Type 316 stainless steel exposed to a fuel salt in a type 316 stainless steel loop showed a maximum uniform corrosion rate of 1 mil/year for 4298 hr.
